title: The Best Pharma Stock I’ve Ever Seen

transcript.text: If you knew a loved one was going to die
and there was no cure for their illness,
do you think you could build one? Seems
like an impossible task, right? Which is
why the founding of today's company is a
story in itself. Let's set aside for a
second how wildly profitable this
company is, how strong their revenue
growth is, how they're truly focused on
helping others. Forget about visions of
a solar powered pig organ harvesting
farm, of drones landing on helipads
transporting organs to people who need
them. I want you to focus for a second
on this woman, Martine Rothblat. Sorry,
what's that? You had enough of all this
feel-good You just want to
know the ticker. UTR, United
Therapeutics. Off you go now. You're
probably going to miss one of the
funnest presentations I've ever given.
Now, I want you to look at this woman. I
don't know her. I never worked with her.
She could be a total b- word for all I
know.
>> No, you're not.
>> Yes, I am.
>> But when I first came across her
company, I knew she was built
differently. Now, she's a lawyer who
founded SiriusXM Radio in 1990, but she
left her role in that firm, sold off a
bunch of her shares to raise money, and
she started a pharmaceutical company.
That's because her daughter Genesis had
a condition, an illness called pulmonary
hypertension and she had three years
left to live and there was no cure for
her illness. And when Martin asked the
doctor why it was there was no
treatment, the doctor said, "Well,
there's only 3,000 people dying of this
per year and pharmaceutical companies
don't want to make that type of
investment." So guess what? Martine
solved that problem. She started a 20
billion dollar company called United
Therapeutics. The sparkle of a promise
of hope for Genesis is not only keeping
Genesis and other people alive today,
but produces almost a billion half
dollars a year in revenue.
She's now close to a billion dollar
quarterly run rate. She's on her fourth
drug and recently her daughter who works
at United Therapeutics celebrated her
40th birthday there. Now, here's what's
most incredible. Perhaps you remember
those 3,000 people that were dying?
Well, they're not dying anymore, right?
They're living a whole lot longer. So
now it went from 3,000 people dying per
year to 50,000 people being treated a
year by the therapeutics being designed
by Martin. So this is truly an example
of doing well while doing good. Now, I
didn't know any of this when I first
started researching this company nearly
a decade ago, but what I did notice
right away was that this company was
very different. Have you ever seen a
corporate page that looks like this?
Have you ever seen Seale executive bios
that look like this? I love this. We go,
not ego. That sort of theme persists
throughout all of the company's
collateral that you come across at this
page of their board of directors drawn
up beautifully. If you look at one of
the people on here, Ray Kerszswhile,
right? This gentleman inspired me to get
into researching nanotechnology several
decades ago when I found analyze. And if
you look in the upper left corner there,
you see public benefit corporation. Aha.
So, this is something that historically
we've frowned upon because it's the idea
of moving away from adding value to
shareholders and starting to add value
in other places. And I think if that's
misguided, it's quite dangerous. Now
when we look at a company like United
Therapeutics, it really seems to be one
of the cases where it works well. And
first of all, you can see here the share
price appreciation over time. So this
company has returned 10 times the
NASDAQ's return over the entire time
it's been publicly traded. So
shareholders have certainly been
rewarded by Martin. And she has of
course also rewarded herself. And for
the level of competency on exhibit, we
would expect that to be the case. We
certainly see a very well-run business
that's executing quite well. And of
course, immediately when you see a share
price performance like that over 7,000%,
you want to say, well, are the
fundamentals matching the share price
appreciation? So, what sort of growth
would justify this outperformance? when
you look at their revenue growth here. I
love just how smooth this is. So,
typically you see a lot of volatility
with pharma companies, right? Well, this
is nice, smooth, beautiful revenue
growth, but right there in the center,
you see that dip and we investigated
that back in 2021. We've been following
this company for what nearly a decade
focused on their work in xeno
transplantation. Also noting that it's a
rather remarkable business with a very
remarkable story. Now, we noted that
drop was due to falling sales for one of
their flagship drugs. That was due to
pressure from generics. So, like any
other pharma company, they're having to
balance their existing drugs against
drugs coming down the pipeline to keep
the financials somewhat steady over
time. And back then, we pulled up this
table showing the five main drugs that
the business is marketing. And you see
the one there on the bottom that was
getting hit quite hard. Well, when you
look today, what's interesting is you
see those same five drugs, but in a
dominant position there, this Tyaso
represents at least last quarter 60% of
total revenues from two different
formulations. So, we want to immediately
understand more about this key drug and
you can look here. It's a drug of course
that treats pulmonary hypertension and
there's two methods of delivery here.
The first is a nebulizer which is a
rather bulky device that one uses to
inhale a drug and then you have this
standard inhaler. So what's super
interesting about this is they had that
drug initially released on the bulky
nebulizer and then they went and
developed an inhaler formulation and
then that started to sell. Right? So it
didn't just cannibalize the nebulizer
delivery method. The combined sales of
Tyaso across those two delivery methods
actually has been going very strongly
over time. And we recently did a video
on five biotech stocks we like. United
Therapeutics being one of them. And this
gentleman had left a rather verbose
comment pointing to the fact that this
is a very solid growth story even
without the xenotransplantation
thesis. And he cited a number of things
here that investors would want to watch.
They're going to have this once daily
oral treatment that may cannibalize
their existing drugs, but it's going to
stave off competitors. He mentioned a
few names, Liquidia being one of them.
Now, that certainly rang a bell because
I recall back in 2018, we took a look at
the work Liquidia was doing. The
gentleman who founded this company was
actually quite a notable entrepreneur
who dabbled in quite a few businesses in
3D printing as well. And we had
mentioned back, this was a decade ago in
looking at liquidia that their ability
to create particles at the nanometer
level would allow them to create new
delivery methods for drugs. Recall back
then you had FLL and Skyarm and these
companies that were using nanotechnology
for drug delivery. This was more than
two decades ago, right? And some of this
work is quite cool. And here you can see
this piece we did looking specifically
at therapies they're working on. Now,
they're a competitor to United
Therapeutics. We noted that. Here you
can see the baby using the nebulizer.
Now, now I did want to point to one
thing. So, annual premium subscribers
have access to what we call our
disruptive tech stock catalog. It
contains 450 stocks. It's an Excel
spreadsheet. Has a ton of rich
information in there. One of the
companies listed in that spreadsheet is
United Therapeutics and we currently
have them listed as an avoid. I looked
at the note there. We said, "Well, we're
impressed with the growth in
profitability on display, but we need a
breakdown of xenotransplantation
revenues before we consider buying." So,
we're avoiding simply because it doesn't
give us exposure to that thesis. What's
more interesting is we used to actually
hold this company prior to establishing
our 30 stock analyze disruptive tech
portfolio. This didn't make the cut
because it didn't have that exposure. So
let's talk a little bit about the
xenotransplantation thesis that we've
been following for a long time in the
context of United Therapeutics. Very
cool stuff. They have pigs that they've
developed with 10 gene edits. The
problem is that firstly a pig organ is
most suitable to use as a human organ
from a form factor perspective. The
problem is that when you put the pig
organ into the human, the human doesn't
like it. Well, they took a pig and they
edited a whole bunch of genes and now it
seems to work. And they have a whole
bunch of firsts. You may have seen these
mentioned, you know, a CNN article or
BBC article. Oh, a pig organ transplant,
right? Exciting stuff. Well, United
Therapeutics is working on that. It's
very cool. And they've opened up this
facility. I was listening to an
interview with Martin just today and she
was rather optimistic talking about how
this facility they've built is going to
be easily expanded to start churning out
organs fairly quickly. You've got all
these grand plans of helellipads and
she's really big into the carbon-f free
aspect. She's talking about how she's
flying around carbon-f free helicopters.
This woman is doing a whole lot of
stuff. But as always, we don't invest in
stories. What we're primarily interested
in as always, what are the next major
milestones to watch for? So they have
this ongoing expand trial and presumably
after this year they're going to be able
to submit this biologics license
applications. I believe that's one of
the later stage if not one of the last
things they would do before moving to
commercialization. And they're going to
expand their facility so they can do
commercial scale production of pig
organs as I said and they have potential
FDA approval of this Ukidney as their
first xenotransplantation
product. Now I think this company
probably stands on its own as a
potential investment for many and that
xenotransplantation thesis is simply an
added bonus. Now next steps here I think
first of all we want to look a bit into
their profitability over time and just
across the top here you see gross
margins what that's 95% bar on top 85%
bar on the bottom they're sitting within
that range very impressive of course
operating margins will mimic that as
well which then leads us to valuation
right and when we calculate simple
valuation ratio that's just market cap
divided by annualized revenues we get
6.5 well our catalog average is around
7.5 so this wouldn't be considered
overvalued from that perspective. Next,
you'd want to look at price to earnings
since this is quite a profitable firm,
right? You want to evaluate that against
appropriate benchmarks, other pharma
companies, for example, right? And I
think establishing a concise list of
milestones, right? What's the next big
pipeline milestone for this company? And
you want to reduce noise and make that
list as simple as possible. And as
usual, you don't want to inundate
yourself with information. We find that
checking in with companies once per year
is a pretty decent cadence and
understand this whole public benefit
corporation appeal. I think that's
important, right? Make sure that aligns
with your investing aims because anytime
somebody says, "Listen, you as a
shareholder, you're going to take second
place. These other stakeholders, we're
going to put them in front of you." You
want to understand what they mean by
that. And I found her explanation of
that to be quite compelling, almost
something that you would really want to
support, frankly. Now, one thing that's
inherent to pharma companies is a lot of
volatility. So, we're going to go ahead
and move this stock from an avoid to a
like in our catalog. That means if
there's any sort of dramatic share price
volatility that will comment on that for
premium subscribers in our nanalyze
market open, that's an email that goes
out every trading day to paying
subscribers a concise set of
information. It also includes whether or
not we're buying a stock if we ever
decided to enter a position into United
Therapeutics. I don't think that's out
of the question, but I would like to see
a little bit more progress on the
xenotransplantation side still. Now,
I'll leave you with this quote from
Martin. She says, "A leader works for
those who they lead." I love that. But I
will also say that as the leader of
Nanalyze, I not only work for the people
that I lead, we all work for our paying
customers. So, Nanalyze premium
subscribers out there, thank you so much
for your financial support and know that
we never take that for granted. Now, I'm
going to go ahead and leave you with
this piece that we did recently on five
biotech stocks we like, five we don't.
Give that a watch next. Thanks so much
for taking the time to watch this today.
